_comment: Ground truth annotation — CONSORT checklist for ALLHAT 2002 — Gold standard RCT

article.title: Major Outcomes in High-Risk Hypertensive Patients Randomized to Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme Inhibitor or Calcium Channel Blocker vs Diuretic: The Antihypertensive and Lipid-Lowering Treatment to Prevent Heart Attack Trial (ALLHAT)
article.authors: The ALLHAT Officers and Coordinators for the ALLHAT Collaborative Research Group

study_type.notes: Large double-blind active-controlled RCT with 33,357 high-risk hypertensive patients comparing three drug classes (ACE inhibitor, calcium channel blocker, diuretic). Gold standard pragmatic trial. Despite extensive follow-up and cohort language, ALLHAT is an RCT by design with randomization to treatment arms. Design ambiguity with cohort language noted.

1a.notes: Title identifies as randomized trial: 'Randomized to Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme Inhibitor or Calcium Channel Blocker vs Diuretic'

1b.notes: Structured abstract with Context, Objective, Design, Setting and Participants, Interventions, Main Outcomes, Results, Conclusions

2a.notes: Introduction provides scientific background: cost of hypertension treatment, benefit of BP lowering, uncertainty about optimal drug class, prior evidence for ACE inhibitors and CCBs

2b.notes: Objective explicitly stated: 'determine whether treatment with a calcium channel blocker or an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor lowers the incidence of coronary heart disease or other cardiovascular disease events vs treatment with a diuretic'

3a.notes: Trial design described: 'randomized, double-blind, active-controlled clinical trial' conducted February 1994 through March 2002; allocation to three groups (chlorthalidone, amlodipine, lisinopril)

3b.notes: Protocol amendments mentioned: primary outcome and study design modifications during trial; doxazosin arm discontinued early due to increased heart failure risk

4a.notes: Eligibility criteria: age 55 or older, hypertension, at least 1 other CHD risk factor (prior MI, LVH, diabetes, smoking, lipid abnormality)

4b.notes: Settings: 623 North American clinical centers (offices, clinics, HMOs) in United States and Canada

5.notes: Interventions detailed: chlorthalidone 12.5-25 mg/d, amlodipine 2.5-10 mg/d, lisinopril 10-40 mg/d; dosing titrated to BP targets; background medications allowed as needed

6a.notes: Primary outcome: coronary heart disease (CHD) event or stroke (first hospitalization for MI or CHD death or fatal/nonfatal stroke). Secondary outcomes: all-cause mortality, combined cardiovascular disease events

6b.notes: Changes to outcomes documented: primary outcome expanded during trial; some secondary outcomes modified

7a.notes: Sample size calculation described: power calculation for detecting 10% reduction in CHD outcome; 33,357 participants enrolled to achieve adequate power (>85%)

7b.notes: Interim analyses and stopping rules: doxazosin arm discontinued by DSMB due to increased heart failure risk

8a.notes: Random sequence generation: computerized randomization system described; method used to generate allocation sequence

8b.notes: Type of randomization: stratified randomization by clinical center; 1:1:1 allocation to three drug treatment groups

9.notes: Allocation concealment: central randomization system at coordinating center; local staff could not predict treatment assignment

10.notes: Implementation: local clinic staff obtained allocation assignments from computerized randomization system after enrollment; sequence generation and allocation separate

11a.notes: Blinding: double-blind described; patients and study staff blinded to treatment assignment (chlorthalidone vs. amlodipine vs. lisinopril masked by identical capsules)

11b.notes: Similarity of interventions: all three active drugs delivered in identical-appearing capsules; matching placebo design employed to maintain blinding

12a.notes: Statistical methods: survival analysis (life-table estimates), Kaplan-Meier curves, log-rank tests, Cox proportional hazards models for estimating hazard ratios with 95% CIs

12b.notes: Additional analyses: subgroup analyses by race (African American, Caucasian, Hispanic, Asian), age group, sex, diabetes status; sensitivity analyses; interaction tests

13a.notes: Participant flow: Figure 1 shows CONSORT flow diagram with numbers randomized (33,357), allocated, and analyzed by group; enrollment and follow-up process clearly diagrammed

13b.notes: Losses and exclusions after randomization: numbers discontinuing due to intolerance, poor compliance, relocated, lost to follow-up documented by treatment group

14a.notes: Recruitment period: February 1994 through March 2002 (approximately 8-year recruitment); mean follow-up 4.9 years

14b.notes: Trial ended: planned duration completed; doxazosin arm discontinued early (1999) by DSMB recommendation due to safety findings

15.notes: Baseline characteristics: Table 1 presents demographic and clinical characteristics by treatment group (age, race, BP, lipids, comorbidities, prior CV events)

16.notes: Numbers analyzed: intention-to-treat analysis; all randomized participants included in primary outcome analysis (33,357 total: 15,255 chlorthalidone, 9,048 amlodipine, 9,054 lisinopril)

17a.notes: Primary outcome results: CHD outcomes and hazard ratios by group (e.g., 2,121 events); rates per 1000 person-years with 95% confidence intervals; log-rank p-values

17b.notes: Both relative hazard ratios and absolute event rates reported for primary outcome and major secondary outcomes by treatment group

18.notes: Subgroup analyses extensively reported: results stratified by race, age, sex, diabetes, LVH status showing treatment interactions and differential effects

19.notes: Adverse effects reported: doxazosin discontinued due to increased heart failure; hyperkalemia with ACE inhibitors; cough with lisinopril; ankle edema with amlodipine; hypokalemia with chlorthalidone

20.notes: Limitations discussed: open follow-up for lifestyle/other medications, loss to follow-up, potential confounding, population predominantly older and with high CV risk, lack of generalizability to younger patients

21.notes: Generalizability addressed: applicability to high-risk hypertensive patients age 55+; predominantly included older patients and those with existing CVD risk factors; limited in younger, lower-risk populations

22.notes: Interpretation: chlorthalidone and amlodipine produced similar outcomes; lisinopril inferior; discussion of mechanisms and clinical implications for antihypertensive therapy selection

23.notes: Registration: NCT number provided in methods section; trial registered with clinical trials registry

24.notes: Protocol available: full protocol published separately; study protocol available to readers

25.notes: Funding: National Institutes of Health (NHLBI) and participating institutions; funders' roles in study design, conduct, analysis, and publication decision described
